paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the mid-1700s and by the 1830s and 1840s had spread to many other parts of the world, including the United States. In Britain, it was a period when a largely rural, agrarian* society was transformed into an industrialised, urban one. Goods that had once been crafted by hand started to be produced in mass quantities by machines in factories, thanks to the invention of steam power and the introduction of new machines and manufacturing techniques in textiles, iron-making and other industries.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: The foundations of the Industrial Revolution date back to the early 1700s, when the English inventor Thomas Newcomen designed the first modern steam engine. Called the "atmospheric steam engine", Newcomen's invention was originally used to power machines that pumped water out of mines. In the 1760s, the Scottish engineer James Watt started to adapt one of Newcomen's models, and succeeded in making it far more efficient. Watt later worked with the English manufacturer Matthew Boulton to invent a new steam engine driven by both the forward and backward strokes of the piston, while the gear mechanism it was connected to produced rotary motion. It was a key innovation that would allow steam power to spread across British industries.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: The demand for coal, which was a relatively cheap energy source, grew rapidly during the Industrial Revolution, as it was needed to run not only the factories used to produce manufactured goods, but also steam-powered transportation. In the early 1800s, the English engineer Richard Trevithick built a steam-powered locomotive, and by 1830 goods and passengers were being transported between the industrial centres of Manchester and Liverpool. In addition, steam-powered boats and ships were widely used to carry goods along Britain's canals as well as across the Atlantic.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Britain had produced textiles like wool, linen and cotton for hundreds of years, but prior to the Industrial Revolution, the British textile business was a true "cottage industry", with the work performed in small workshops or even homes by individual spinners, weavers and dyers. Starting in the mid-1700s, innovations like the spinning jenny and the power loom made weaving cloth and spinning yarn and thread much easier. With these machines, relatively little labour was required to produce cloth, and the new, mechanised textile factories that opened around the country were quickly able to meet customer demand for cloth both at home and abroad.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: The British iron industry also underwent major change as it adopted new innovations. Chief among the new techniques was the smelting of iron ore with coke (a material made by heating coal) instead of the traditional charcoal. This method was cheaper and produced metals that were of a higher quality, enabling Britain's iron and steel production to expand in response to demand created by the Napoleonic Wars (1803–15) and the expansion of the railways from the 1830s.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: The latter part of the Industrial Revolution also saw key advances in communication methods, as people increasingly saw the need to communicate efficiently over long distances. In 1837, British inventors William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone patented the first commercial telegraphy system. In the 1830s and 1840s, Samuel Morse and other inventors worked on their own versions in the United States. Cooke and Wheatstone's system was soon used for railway signalling in the UK. As the speed of the new locomotives increased, it was essential to have a fast and effective means of avoiding collisions.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: The impact of the Industrial Revolution on people's lives was immense. Although many people in Britain had begun moving to the cities from rural areas before the Industrial Revolution, this accelerated dramatically with industrialisation, as the rise of large factories turned smaller towns into major cities in just a few decades. This rapid urbanisation brought significant challenges, as overcrowded cities suffered from pollution and inadequate sanitation.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle. Factory workers had to work long hours in dangerous conditions for extremely low wages. These conditions along with the rapid pace of change fuelled opposition to industrialisation. A group of British workers who became known as "Luddites" were British weavers and textile workers who objected to the increased use of mechanised looms and knitting frames. Many had spent years learning their craft, and they feared that unskilled machine operators were robbing them of their livelihood. A few desperate weavers began breaking into factories and smashing textile machines. They called themselves Luddites after Ned Ludd, a young apprentice who was rumoured to have wrecked a textile machine in 1779.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: The first major instances of machine breaking took place in 1811 in the city of Nottingham, and the practice soon spread across the country. Machine-breaking Luddites attacked and burned factories, and in some cases they even exchanged gunfire with company guards and soldiers. The workers wanted employers to stop installing new machinery, but the British government responded to the uprisings by making machine-breaking punishable by death. The unrest finally reached its peak in April 1812, when a few Luddites were shot during an attack on a mill near Huddersfield. In the days that followed, other Luddites were arrested, and dozens were hanged or transported to Australia. By 1813, the Luddite resistance had all but vanished.

paragraphs.label: J
paragraphs.text: * agrarian: relating to the land, especially the use of land for farming

groups.title: Britain's Industrial Revolution
groups.desc:
Questions 1-7

Complete the notes below.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 1-7 on your answer sheet.
groups.collect: Steam power • Newcomen's steam engine was used in mines to remove water. • In Watt and Boulton's steam engine, the movement of the $34039$ was linked to a gear system. • A greater supply of $34040$ was required to power steam engines. Textile industry • Before the Industrial Revolution, spinners and weavers worked at home and in $34041$ • Not as much $34042$ was needed to produce cloth once the spinning jenny and power loom were invented. Iron industry • Smelting of iron ore with coke resulted in material that was better $34043$. • Demand for iron increased with the growth of the $34044$. Communications • Cooke and Wheatstone patented the first telegraphy system. • The telegraphy system was used to prevent locomotives colliding. Urbanisation • Small towns turned into cities very quickly. • The new cities were dirty, crowded and lacked sufficient $34045$.

questions.analysis: 正确答案是“piston”，因为题干中的“movement of the 【 】 was linked to a gear system”在文章中最直接对应的表达是“the forward and backward strokes of the piston, while the gear mechanism it was connected to”。通过定位关键词和理解句子结构，即使词汇量有限的学生也可以找到正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: Watt later worked with the English manufacturer Matthew Boulton to invent a new steam engine driven by both the forward and backward strokes of the piston, while the gear mechanism it was connected to produced rotary motion.
central_sentences.translation: 瓦特后来与英国制造商马修·博尔顿合作，发明了一种新的蒸汽机，这种蒸汽机由活塞的前后冲程驱动，而与之相连的齿轮机构则产生旋转运动。

questions.analysis: 正确答案是 "coal"。题干中的 "supply" 对应原文中的 "demand"，"steam engines" 对应原文中的 "steam-powered"。原文明确指出 "The demand for coal... grew rapidly... as it was needed to run... steam-powered transportation"，这表明煤炭是驱动蒸汽机所需的能源。因此，答案是 "coal"。

central_sentences.sentence: The demand for coal, which was a relatively cheap energy source, grew rapidly during the Industrial Revolution, as it was needed to run not only the factories used to produce manufactured goods, but also steam-powered transportation.
central_sentences.translation: 对煤炭的需求在工业革命期间迅速增长，因为它不仅用于生产制造商品的工厂，还用于蒸汽动力的交通工具。

In addition, steam-powered boats and ships were widely used to carry goods along Britain's canals as well as across the Atlantic.

central_sentences.sentence: Britain had produced textiles like wool, linen and cotton, for hundreds of years, but prior to the Industrial Revolution, the British textile business was a true "cottage industry", with the work performed in small workshops or even homes by individual spinners, weavers and dyers.
central_sentences.translation: 英国已经生产了数百年的羊毛、亚麻和棉花等纺织品，但在工业革命之前，英国的纺织业是真正的“家庭工业”，工作由个别纺纱工、织布工和染色工在小作坊甚至家中进行。

questions.analysis: 题目要求填空的内容是“在发明纺纱珍妮机和动力织布机之后，生产布料所需要的某种资源减少了”。根据文章中的句子“relatively little labour was required to produce cloth”，很明显可以知道答案是“labour”。 题干的“不需要太多”与文章中的“相对较少”是同义替换；而“was needed”与“was required”也是同义替换。因此，答案填入“labour”或“labor”是正确的。

central_sentences.sentence: With these machines, relatively little labour was required to produce cloth, and the new, mechanised textile factories that opened around the country were quickly able to meet customer demand for cloth both at home and abroad.

central_sentences.sentence: Chief among the new techniques was the smelting of iron ore with coke (a material made by heating coal) instead of the traditional charcoal. This method was cheaper and produced metals that were of a higher quality, enabling Britain's iron and steel production to expand in response to demand created by the Napoleonic Wars (1803-15) and the expansion of the railways from the 1830s.
central_sentences.translation: 新技术中最重要的是用焦炭（通过加热煤炭制成的材料）而不是传统的木炭来冶炼铁矿石。这种方法更便宜，并且生产出质量更高的金属，使英国的铁和钢生产能够应对拿破仑战争（1803-15）和1830年代铁路扩展所带来的需求。

groups.desc:
Questions 8-13

Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1?

In boxes 8-13 on your answer sheet, write

TRUE if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this

questions.title: Britain's canal network grew rapidly so that more goods could be transported around the country.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到了“Britain's canal network grew rapidly”，而原文中的表述只是指出了蒸汽船被用于沿着运河运输货物，但并未提及运河网络是否有快速增长的情况。由于文章中缺乏与题干中“grew rapidly”这一概念对应的信息，我们不能确认运河网络是否迅速发展。因此，正确答案为NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: In addition, steam-powered boats and ships were widely used to carry goods along Britain's canals as well as across the Atlantic.
central_sentences.translation: 此外，蒸汽动力的船只被广泛用来沿着英国的运河运输货物，以及横渡大西洋。

target_sen.target_sen: In addition, steam-powered boats and ships were widely used to carry goods along Britain's canals as well as across the Atlantic.

questions.title: Costs in the iron industry rose when the technique of smelting iron ore with coke was introduced.
questions.analysis: 文章中提到了引入使用焦炭冶炼铁矿石这种技术的铁工业，称这种方法更便宜，并且生产了更高质量的金属。这与题干中提到的“铁工业的成本上升”相矛盾，因为“更便宜”的意思是成本下降而不是上升。因此，答案是“FALSE”。

central_sentences.sentence: This method was cheaper and produced metals that were of a higher quality, enabling
central_sentences.translation: 这种方法更便宜，并且生产出了质量更高的金属...

target_sen.target_sen: This method was cheaper and produced metals that were of a higher quality, enabling Britain's iron and steel production to expand in response to demand created by the Napoleonic Wars (1803-15) and the expansion of the railways from the 1830s.
The British iron industry also underwent major change as it adopted new innovations.

syn_ary.synonym: smelting of iron ore with coke

target_sen.target_sen: Chief among the new techniques was the smelting of iron ore with coke (a material made by heating coal) instead of the traditional charcoal.

questions.title: Samuel Morse's communication system was more reliable than that developed by William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到的 Samuel Morse 和 William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone 的通讯系统在原文中都有提及，但原文并没有提到两者系统的可靠性比较。因此，题干中的信息在原文中找不到，答案为 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: In the 1830s and 1840s, Samuel Morse and other inventors worked on their own versions in the United States. Cooke and Wheatstone's system was soon used for railway signalling in the UK.
central_sentences.translation: 在1830年代和1840年代，Samuel Morse和其他发明家在美国开发了他们自己的版本。Cooke和Wheatstone的系统很快在英国用于铁路信号传输。

target_sen.target_sen: In the 1830s and 1840s, Samuel Morse and other inventors worked on their own versions in the United States.
In 1837, British inventors William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone patented the first commercial telegraphy system.
In 1837, British inventors William Cooke and Charles Wheatstone patented the first commercial telegraphy system.

questions.title: The economic benefits of industrialisation were limited to certain sectors of society.
questions.analysis: 答案是“TRUE”是因为文章明确指出工业化的经济利益主要集中在中产和上层阶级，中产和上层阶级的生活水平得到了改善，但许多穷人仍在挣扎。这证明经济利益并非普遍分布，而是“limited to certain sectors of society”，与题干完全一致。因此答案为“TRUE”。

central_sentences.sentence: Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle.
central_sentences.translation: 虽然工业化总体上提高了国家的经济产出，并改善了中产阶级和上层阶级的生活水平，但许多穷人仍在挣扎。

target_sen.target_sen: Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle.
Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle.
Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle.
Although industrialisation increased the country's economic output overall and improved the standard of living for the middle and upper classes, many poor people continued to struggle.

questions.title: Some skilled weavers believed that the introduction of the new textile machines would lead to job losses.
questions.analysis: 本题的正确答案是 TRUE，因为文章明确提到了一些熟练的织工（Luddites）担心新纺织机器的引入会导致他们失去工作（"robbing them of their livelihood"）。通过识别题干和文章中的同义表达，并理解核心意思，学生可以正确判断答案。

central_sentences.sentence: A group of British workers who became known as "Luddites" were British weavers and textile workers who objected to the increased use of mechanised looms and knitting frames. Many had spent years learning their craft, and they feared that unskilled machine operators were robbing them of their livelihood.
central_sentences.translation: 一群被称为“卢德派”的英国工人是英国的织工和纺织工人，他们反对机械织机和针织机的增加使用。许多人花了多年时间学习他们的手艺，他们担心不熟练的机器操作员会剥夺他们的生计。

target_sen.target_sen: A group of British workers who became known as "Luddites" were British weavers and textile workers who objected to the increased use of mechanised looms and knitting frames.

syn_ary.synonym: increased use of mechanised looms and knitting frames

target_sen.target_sen: A group of British workers who became known as "Luddites" were British weavers and textile workers who objected to the increased use of mechanised looms and knitting frames.

locating_words.locating_word: introduction of the new textile machines

target_sen.target_sen: Many had spent years learning their craft, and they feared that unskilled machine operators were robbing them of their livelihood.

questions.title: There was some sympathy among local people for the Luddites who were arrested near Huddersfield.
questions.analysis: 根据文章内容，该段落描述了在哈德斯菲尔德附近发生的“卢德派”事件以及他们被捕的结局，但并没有提到“当地人对被捕卢德派的同情”的信息。对于判断题来说，NOT GIVEN 的意思是文章没有提到相关信息，因此根据文章内容和题干的对比，这道题的答案为 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: The unrest finally reached its peak in April 1812, when a few Luddites were shot during an attack on a mill near Huddersfield. In the days that followed, other Luddites were arrested, and dozens were hanged or transported to Australia.
central_sentences.translation: 骚乱最终在1812年4月达到了顶峰，那时有几名“卢德派”在哈德斯菲尔德附近一次工厂攻击中被枪杀。在随后的几天里，其他“卢德派”被捕，几十人被绞死或流放到澳大利亚。

target_sen.target_sen: Machine-breaking Luddites attacked and burned factories, and in some cases they even exchanged gunfire with company guards and soldiers.
The unrest finally reached its peak in April 1812, when a few Luddites were shot during an attack on a mill near Huddersfield.
In the days that followed, other Luddites were arrested, and dozens were hanged or transported to Australia.

content.title: Athletes and stress
content.summary: {'A': '成为职业运动员并不容易，不仅身体要求极高，还面临比赛时的巨大心理压力，英国网球选手艾玛·拉杜卡努在 2021 年温网退赛后在社交媒体上提到了这一点。', 'B': '运动员会感受到一定程度的表现压力，其压力反应取决于需求和资源两个因素，需求包括成功所需的高强度身心努力等，资源则是应对需求的能力。', 'C': '新需求或情况变化会影响人对压力的正负反应，更多资源会带来积极反应即挑战状态，过多需求则导致消极反应即威胁状态，艾玛·拉杜卡努在温网可能因需求大资源不足而经历威胁反应。', 'D': '挑战和威胁反应本质上影响身体对压力情况的反应，会影响肾上腺素和皮质醇的产生，挑战状态有利于运动表现，威胁状态则相反，会使人心率加快等。', 'E': '焦虑是运动员在压力下的常见体验，会导致心率加快等多种症状，其强度取决于需求和资源，负面压力反应对身心健康有害。', 'F': '运动员可通过多种方式确保在压力下积极反应，如语言、心理技巧如可视化等，训练中创造竞争压力也有帮助。'}

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: It isn't easy being a professional athlete. Not only are the physical demands greater than most people could handle, athletes also face intense psychological pressure during competition. This is something that British tennis player Emma Raducanu wrote about on social media following her withdrawal from the 2021 Wimbledon tournament. Though the young player had been doing well in the tournament, she began having difficulty regulating her breathing and heart rate during a match, which she later attributed to "the accumulation of the excitement and the buzz."

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: For athletes, some level of performance stress is almost unavoidable. But there are many different factors that dictate just how people's minds and bodies respond to stressful events. Typically, stress is the result of an exchange between two factors: demands and resources. An athlete may feel stressed about an event if they feel the demands on them are greater than they can handle. These demands include the high level of physical and mental effort required to succeed, and also the athlete's concerns about the difficulty of the event, their chance of succeeding, and any potential dangers such as injury. Resources, on the other hand, are a person's ability to cope with these demands. These include factors such as the competitor's degree of confidence, how much they believe they can control the situation's outcome, and whether they're looking forward to the event or not.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Each new demand or change in circumstances affects whether a person responds positively or negatively to stress. Typically, the more resources a person feels they have in handling the situation, the more positive their stress response. This positive stress response is called a challenge state. But should the person feel there are too many demands placed on them, the more likely they are to experience a negative stress response—known as a threat state. Research shows that challenge states lead to good performance, while threat states lead to poorer performance. So, in Emma Raducanu's case, a much larger audience, higher expectations and facing a more skilful opponent, may all have led her to feel there were greater demands being placed on her at Wimbledon—but she didn't have the resources to tackle them. This led to her experiencing a threat response.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: Our challenge and threat responses essentially influence how our body responds to stressful situations, as both affect the production of adrenaline and cortisol—also known as 'stress hormones.' During a challenge state, adrenaline increases the amount of blood pumped from the heart and expands the blood vessels, which allows more energy to be delivered to the muscles and brain. This increase of blood and decrease of pressure in the blood vessels has been consistently related to superior sport performance in everything from cricket batting, to golf putting and football penalty taking. But during a threat state, cortisol inhibits the positive effect of adrenaline, resulting in tighter blood vessels, higher blood pressure, slower psychological responses, and a faster heart rate. In short, a threat state makes people more anxious—they make worse decisions and perform more poorly. In tennis players, cortisol has been associated with more unsuccessful serves and greater anxiety.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: That said, anxiety is also a common experience for athletes when they're under pressure. Anxiety can increase heart rate and perspiration, cause heart palpitations, muscle tremors and shortness of breath, as well as headaches, nausea, stomach pain, weakness and a desire to escape in more extreme cases. Anxiety can also reduce concentration and self-control and cause overthinking. The intensity with which a person experiences anxiety depends on the demands and resources they have. Anxiety may also manifest itself in the form of excitement or nervousness depending on the stress response. Negative stress responses can be damaging to both physical and mental health—and repeated episodes of anxiety coupled with negative responses can increase risk of heart disease and depression.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: But there are many ways athletes can ensure they respond positively under pressure. Positive stress responses can be promoted through the language that they and others—such as coaches or parents—use. Psychologists can also help athletes change how they see their physiological responses—such as helping them see a higher heart rate as excitement, rather than nerves. Developing psychological skills, such as visualisation, can also help decrease physiological responses to threat. Visualisation may involve the athlete recreating a mental picture of a time when they performed well, or picturing themselves doing well in the future. This can help create a feeling of control over the stressful event. Recreating competitive pressure during training can also help athletes learn how to deal with stress. An example of this might be scoring athletes against their peers to create a sense of competition. This would increase the demands which players experience compared to a normal training session, while still allowing them to practise coping with stress.

content.title: An inquiry into the existence of the gifted child

paragraphs.label: A
paragraphs.text: Let us start by looking at a modern "genius", Maryam Mirzakhani, who died at the early age of 40. She was the only woman to win the Fields Medal - the mathematical equivalent of a Nobel prize. It would be easy to assume that someone as special as Mirzakhani must have been one of those "gifted" children, those who have an extraordinary ability in a specific sphere of activity or knowledge. But look closer and a different story emerges. Mirzakhani was born in Tehran, Iran. She went to a highly selective girls' school but maths wasn't her interest - reading was. She loved novels and would read anything she could lay her hands on. As for maths, she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he'd learned. He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked.

paragraphs.label: B
paragraphs.text: In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks. One of her comments sums it up. "Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But most of the time, doing mathematics for me is like being on a long hike with no trail and no end in sight." That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.

paragraphs.label: C
paragraphs.text: Is her background unusual? Apparently not. Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood. Einstein was slow to talk as a baby. He failed the general part of the entry test to Zurich Polytechnic - though they let him in because of high physics and maths scores. He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.

paragraphs.label: D
paragraphs.text: There has been a considerable amount of research on high performance over the last century that suggests it goes way beyond tested intelligence. On top of that, research is clear that brains are flexible, new neural pathways can be created, and IQ isn't fixed. For example, just because you can read stories with hundreds of pages at the age of five doesn't mean you will still be ahead of your contemporaries in your teens.

paragraphs.label: E
paragraphs.text: While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught - even traits such as curiosity. According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I've collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented. However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers - curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example - an approach Eyre calls "high performance learning". Critically, they need the right support in developing those approaches at home as well as at school.

paragraphs.label: F
paragraphs.text: Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise. After research going back to 1980 into diverse achievements, from music to memory to sport, he doesn't think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance. Deliberate practice, that stretches you every step of the way, and around 10,000 hours of it, is what produces the goods. It's not a magic number - the highest performers move on to doing a whole lot more, of course. Ericsson's memory research is particularly interesting because random students, trained in memory techniques for the study, went on to outperform others thought to have innately superior memories - those who you might call gifted.

paragraphs.label: G
paragraphs.text: But it is perhaps the work of Benjamin Bloom, another distinguished American educationist working in the 1980s, that gives the most pause for thought. Bloom's team looked at a group of extraordinarily high achieving people in disciplines as varied as ballet, swimming, piano, tennis, maths, sculpture and neurology. He found a pattern of parents encouraging and supporting their children, often in areas they enjoyed themselves. Bloom's outstanding people had worked very hard and consistently at something they had become hooked on when at a young age, and their parents all emerged as having strong work ethics themselves.

paragraphs.label: H
paragraphs.text: Eyre says we know how high performers learn. From that she has developed a high performing learning approach. She is working on this with a group of schools, both in Britain and abroad. Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable. Half were getting free school meals because of poverty, more than half were living with a single parent, and four in five were living in disadvantaged areas. Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community. Children talked about the need to work hard at school, to listen in class and keep trying.

paragraphs.label: I
paragraphs.text: Let us end with Einstein, the epitome of a genius. He clearly had curiosity, character and determination. He struggled against rejection in early life but was undeterred. Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: "It's not that I'm so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character."

However, maths held little $34066$ for her as a child and in fact her performance was below average until she was $34067$ by a difficult puzzle that one of her siblings showed her.

Later, as a professional mathematician, she had an inquiring mind and proved herself to be $34068$ when things did not go smoothly. She said she got the greatest $34069$ from making ground-breaking discoveries and in fact she was responsible for some extremely $34070$ mathematical studies.

central_sentences.translation: 她是唯一一位获得菲尔兹奖章的女性——数学界的诺贝尔奖。

target_sen.target_sen: She was the only woman to win the Fields Medal - the mathematical equivalent of a Nobel prize.

questions.analysis: 根据题目的描述 "maths held little 【 】 for her"，并结合文章内容，可以看到文章中明确提到 "maths wasn't her interest - reading was"，这说明数学对她没有吸引力。题干中的 "little" 和 "wasn't" 以及 "appeal" 和 "interest" 是同义替换关系，因此正确答案是 "appeal"。

central_sentences.sentence: She went to a highly selective girls" school but maths wasn"t her interest - reading was. She
central_sentences.translation: 她上了一所高度挑选的女子学校，但她对数学没有兴趣——她的兴趣是阅读。

questions.analysis: 题干中的 "performance was below average" 对应了原文中的 "did rather poorly at it"，而 "until she was ... by a difficult puzzle" 对应了原文中的 "fascinated her" 和 "she was hooked"。 将同义替换词考虑到一起，答案自然是 "intrigued"，因为 "intrigued" 与 "fascinated" 和 "hooked" 都表示对某事产生极大兴趣，被迷住。

central_sentences.sentence: she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he"d learned. He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked
central_sentences.translation: 她在初中的头几年数学成绩相当差，但当她的哥哥告诉她他学到的东西时，她开始感兴趣了。他从杂志上分享了一道著名的数学题，这道题让她很感兴趣——她被迷住了。

target_sen.target_sen: As for maths, she did rather poorly at it for the first couple of years in her middle school, but became interested when her elder brother told her about what he"d learned.
He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked.
He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked.
He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked.
He shared a famous maths problem from a magazine that fascinated her - and she was hooked.

questions.analysis: 题干中的“inquiring mind”在原文中对应“curious”，“when things did not go smoothly”对应“in the face of setbacks”。原文中提到她在面对挫折时表现得非常坚定（resolute），而“determined”是“resolute”的同义词，因此正确答案是“determined”。

central_sentences.sentence: In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks.
central_sentences.translation: 在成年生活中，很明显她对自己所做的事情充满好奇和兴奋，并且在面对挫折时表现得非常坚定。

target_sen.target_sen: In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks.
In adult life it is clear that she was curious, excited by what she did and also resolute in the face of setbacks.

locating_words.locating_word: when things did not go smoothly

questions.analysis: 题干中“the greatest”和文章中“the most rewarding”是同义替换关系，文章中提到Mirzakhani认为“最令人满足的部分是发现和理解新事物的兴奋和享受”，可以对应题干中的“greatest 【 】”。因此，正确答案是J: satisfaction。

central_sentences.sentence: Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But
central_sentences.translation: “当然，最令人满足的部分是“啊哈”时刻，发现和理解新事物的兴奋和享受……”

target_sen.target_sen: "Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new .
"Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new .
"Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new .

questions.analysis: 正确答案是 "I: innovative"。原因在于题干的"ground-breaking discoveries" 和文章中的"the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new" 以及 "original research into mathematics" 都暗示了数学研究的创新性和独特性。在选项中，"innovative" 最能表达这种创新和突破的意思。

central_sentences.sentence: "Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new . . . But most of the time, doing mathematics for me is like being on a long hike with no trail and no end in sight." That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.
central_sentences.translation: “当然，最令人回报的一部分是‘啊哈’时刻，发现的兴奋和理解新事物的乐趣……但大多数时候，对我来说做数学就像是在没有路径和没有尽头的长途跋涉。” 那条道路把她带到了原创数学研究的高峰。

syn_ary.synonym: the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new

target_sen.target_sen: "Of course, the most rewarding part is the “Aha” moment, the excitement of discovery and enjoyment of understanding something new .
That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.
That trail took her to the heights of original research into mathematics.

groups.desc:
Questions 33-37

Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in Reading Passage 3?
In boxes 33-37 on your answer sheet, write
YES if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this

questions.title: Many people who ended up winning prestigious intellectual prizes only reached an average standard when young.
questions.analysis: 题干中的“Many people who ended up winning prestigious intellectual prizes only reached an average standard when young”意思是“许多最终赢得了著名知识奖项的人在年轻时只达到了普通的标准”。在原文中，句子“Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood”直接对应了题干中的意思，说明大多数诺贝尔奖得主在童年时期并不出色。因此，题干中的信息在文章中可以找到，答案是“YES”。

central_sentences.sentence: Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood.
central_sentences.translation: 大多数诺贝尔奖得主在童年时期并不出色。

target_sen.target_sen: Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood.
Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood.
Most Nobel prize winners were unexceptional in childhood.

questions.title: Einstein's failures as a young man were due to his lack of confidence.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到"Einstein's failures as a young man were due to his lack of confidence"。文章确实提到他在开始工作时遇到了困难（"He struggled at work initially"），但并没有提供任何信息说明他的失败是因为缺乏自信导致的（"confidence"）。这在文章中并未得到证实或否定，所以答案是 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.sentence: He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.
central_sentences.translation: 他开始工作时确实遇到了困难，但他不断坚持并最终用他的相对论改写了牛顿力学定律。

central_sentences.sentence: Did he think he was a genius or even gifted? He once wrote: "It's not that I"m so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most
central_sentences.translation: 他认为自己是天才甚至有天赋吗？他曾写道：“不是我很聪明，只是我更坚持与问题作斗争。”

target_sen.target_sen: He struggled at work initially, but he kept plugging away and eventually rewrote the laws of Newtonian mechanics with his theory of relativity.

questions.title: It is difficult to reach agreement on whether some children are actually born gifted.
questions.analysis: 本题的正确答案是 YES，因为文章中明确提到关于天赋是否与生俱来尚未达成一致。题干中的 "difficult to reach agreement" 对应文章中的 "the jury is out on"，而 "born gifted" 对应 "giftedness being innate"。通过识别这些同义表达，可以确定题干信息在文章中是存在的。

central_sentences.sentence: While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference
central_sentences.translation: 关于天赋是否与生俱来尚未达成一致，其他因素可能会有所影响……

target_sen.target_sen: While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught - even traits such as curiosity.

While the jury is out on giftedness being innate and other factors potentially making the difference, what is certain is that the behaviours associated with high levels of performance are replicable and most can be taught - even traits such as curiosity.

questions.title: Einstein was upset by the public's view of his life's work.
questions.analysis: 这道题的正确答案是 NOT GIVEN，因为文章中并没有提供任何关于爱因斯坦对公众对他毕生工作的看法感到沮丧的信息。尽管文章提到了一些关于他生活和工作的细节，如他在早期生活中遭遇的困难和他如何看待自己的聪明才智，但这些都不涉及他对公众看法的感受。因此，这道题的答案应为 NOT GIVEN。

central_sentences.analysis: 只谈其自我看法与character，没提公众。
sentences: Did he think he was a genius or even gifted?
He once wrote: "It's not that I"m so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character."
central_sentences.paraphrasing: upset by public's view -> 原文未提public反应

central_sentences.sentence: Did he think he was a genius or even gifted?
He once wrote: "It's not that I"m so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character."

questions.title: Einstein put his success down to the speed at which he dealt with scientific questions.
questions.analysis: 题干中提到爱因斯坦将他的成功归因于他处理科学问题的速度，而原文中爱因斯坦明确表示他的成功不是因为聪明或速度，而是因为他在问题上坚持得更久，并且认为性格才是关键。因此，题干的断言与原文信息相矛盾，所以答案是NO。

central_sentences.sentence: He once wrote: "It's not that I"m so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer. Most people say it is the intellect which makes a great scientist. They are wrong: it is character
central_sentences.translation: 他曾写道：“不是我很聪明，而是我在问题上坚持得更久。大多数人认为是智力造就了伟大的科学家。他们错了：是性格。”

target_sen.target_sen: He once wrote: "It's not that I"m so smart, it's just that I stay with problems longer.

groups.desc:
Questions 38-40

Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.
Write the correct letter in boxes 38-40 on your answer sheet.

questions.title: What does Eyre believe is needed for children to equal 'gifted' standards?

options.text: strict discipline from the teaching staff
assistance from their peers in the classroom
the development of a spirit of inquiry towards their studies
the determination to surpass everyone else's achievements

questions.analysis: Deborah Eyre认为，为了达到‘天才’标准的水平，学生需要被教授正确的态度和学习方法，并且需要发展与高水平表现者相关的特质，诸如好奇心、坚持和努力工作。在选项C中的“the development of a spirit of inquiry towards their studies”与文中的“curiosity”对应，因此C是正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: However, they must be taught the right attitudes and approaches to their learning and develop the attributes of high performers - curiosity, persistence and hard work, for example - an approach Eyre calls "high performance learning".
central_sentences.translation: 然而，必须教导他们正确的态度和学习方法，并发展高水平表现者的特质—例如好奇心、坚持和努力工作—这是艾尔所称呼的“高效学习”方法。

target_sen.target_sen: According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I"ve collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented.

According to my colleague Prof Deborah Eyre, with whom I"ve collaborated on the book Great Minds and How to Grow Them, the latest neuroscience and psychological research suggests most individuals can reach levels of performance associated in school with the gifted and talented.

questions.title: What is the result of Ericsson's research?

options.text: Very gifted students do not need to work on improving memory skills.
Being born with a special gift is not the key factor in becoming expert.
Including time for physical exercise is crucial in raising performance.
10,000 hours of relevant and demanding work will create a genius.

questions.analysis: 选项B“Being born with a special gift is not the key factor in becoming expert”与原文中的Prof Anders Ericsson的观点“he doesn"t think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance”意思相同，都在强调天生的特殊才能并非成为专家的关键因素。这是明确的对应关系，因此B是正确答案。

central_sentences.sentence: he doesn"t think unique and innate talents are at the heart of performance.
central_sentences.translation: 他不认为独特和天赋的才能是表现的核心。

target_sen.target_sen: Prof Anders Ericsson, an eminent education psychologist at Florida State University, US, is the co-author of Peak: Secrets from the New Science of Expertise.

questions.title: In the penultimate paragraph, it is stated the key to some deprived children's success is

options.text: regular and nourishing diet at home.
the loving support of more than one parent.
a community which has well-funded facilities for learning.
the guidance of someone who recognises the benefits of learning.

questions.analysis: 题干问的是“在倒数第二段中，某些贫困儿童成功的关键是什么？”原文中提到“访谈揭示了在孩子生活中有一个或多个重视和支持教育的成年人，不论是在直系或扩展家庭，还是在孩子的更广泛社区中。”这与选项D中的“the guidance of someone who recognises the benefits of learning”意思相同。因此，正确答案是D。

central_sentences.sentence: Interviews uncovered strong evidence of an adult or adults in the child's life who valued and supported education, either in the immediate or extended family or in the child's wider community.
central_sentences.translation: 访谈揭示了在孩子生活中有一个或多个重视和支持教育的成年人，不论是在直系或扩展家庭，还是在孩子的更广泛社区中。

target_sen.target_sen: Some spin-off research, which looked in detail at 24 of the 3,000 children being studied who were succeeding despite difficult circumstances, found something remarkable.
